perturbation.original_text: 21.2 Dispute Resolution. If there is a disagreement between the PPI and EKR on the interpretation of this Agreement or any aspect of the performance by either Party of its obligations under this Agreement, the Parties shall resolve the dispute in accordance with the dispute resolution procedure set out in Schedule VIII.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.7 Dispute Resolution. If there is a disagreement between the PPI and EKR on the interpretation of this Agreement or any aspect of the performance by either Party of its obligations under this Agreement, the Parties shall resolve the dispute in accordance with the dispute resolution procedure set out in Schedule VIII.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the dispute resolution clause to a section that is unrelated creates a structural flaw and reduces the enforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Federal Arbitration Act (FAA) and general contract law principles favor the enforcement of arbitration agreements, but require that they are not hidden or obscure within a contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: Moving the dispute resolution clause to a random section such as 8.7 reduces its prominence and accessibility, potentially conflicting with FAA requirements that arbitration agreements be clear and not hidden within the contract. This could allow a party to argue the clause is unenforceable because it was not reasonably brought to their attention.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. L. 117–90 inserted “or as otherwise provided in chapter 4” before period at end. Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Effective Date of 2022 Amendment Amendment by Pub. L. 117–90 applicable with respect to any dispute or claim that arises or accrues on or after Mar. 3, 2022 , see section 3 of Pub. Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising ou
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a structural flaw by moving the dispute resolution clause to an unrelated section, thereby potentially making it less prominent and accessible. This conflicts with the principle that arbitration agreements should be clear and not hidden within the contract. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, referencing 9 U.S. Code § 2, emphasize the 'validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate,' implying that such agreements must be presented clearly to be enforceable. Hiding or obscuring such a clause, as implied by the perturbation, undermines this validity by making it difficult for parties to be aware of the dispute resolution process. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: 4.20 Governmental Rebates. Any discounts, rebates, or promotional allowances/incentive programs provided are "discount[s] or other reduction[s] in price" for purposes of 42 U.S.C. Section 1320a-7b(b)(3)(A) and may be subject to the reporting requirements under state and federal Medicaid and Medicare laws. EKR represents that it is aware of its obligations to report discounts resulting from this Agreement to the appropriate reimbursing agencies and authorities (including Medicaid and Medicare). EKR is responsible for complying with and agrees to comply with all applicable requirements, if any, in respect of providing information on such discounts to reimbursing agencies (including Medicaid and Medicare) and other entities in accordance with Applicable Laws and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.6 Governmental Rebates. Any discounts, rebates, or promotional allowances/incentive programs provided are "discount[s] or other reduction[s] in price" for purposes of 42 U.S.C. Section 1320a-7b(b)(3)(A) and may be subject to the reporting requirements under state and federal Medicaid and Medicare laws. EKR represents that it is aware of its obligations to report discounts resulting from this Agreement to the appropriate reimbursing agencies and authorities (including Medicaid and Medicare). EKR is responsible for complying with and agrees to comply with all applicable requirements, if any, in respect of providing information on such discounts to reimbursing agencies (including Medicaid and Medicare) and other entities in accordance with Applicable Laws and regulations.
perturbation.explanation: The relocation of the governmental rebates disclosure to the 'Confidentiality' section makes it less likely to be noticed by individuals reviewing compliance with Medicaid and Medicare regulations. By placing it within confidentiality, which is often regarding trade secrets and private data, it is contradictory to the idea that this data should be accessible to the government.
perturbation.law_explanation: 42 U.S.C. § 1320a-7b(b)(3)(A) deals with illegal remunerations and kickbacks related to federal healthcare programs. By placing the disclosure regarding discounts and rebates under 'Confidentiality,' there is a risk that the disclosure's purpose of ensuring transparency is undermined. This could be interpreted as an attempt to obscure the financial arrangements, potentially violating the spirit of the law, which aims to prevent fraud and abuse in these programs.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: U.S.C. Title 42 - THE PUBLIC HEALTH AND WELFARE 42 U.S.C. United States Code, 2011 Edition Title 42 - THE PUBLIC HEALTH AND WELFARE CHAPTER 7 - SOCIAL SECURITY SUBCHAPTER XI - GENERAL PROVISIONS, PEER REVIEW, AND ADMINISTRATIVE SIMPLIFICATION Part A - General Provisions Sec. 1320a-7b - Criminal penalties for acts involving Federal health care programs From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov §1320a–7b. Criminal penalties for acts involving Federal health care programs (a) Making or causing to be made false statements or representations Whoever— (1) knowingly and willfully makes or causes to be made any false statement or representation of a material fact in any application for any benefit or payment under a Federal health care program (as defined in subsection (f) of this section), (2) at any time knowingly and willfully makes or causes to be made any false statement or representation of a material fact for use in determining rights to such benefit or payment, (3) having knowledge of the occurrence of any event affecting (A) his initial or continued right to any such benefit or payment, or (B) the initial or continued right to any such benefit or payment of any other individual in whose behalf he has applied for or is receiving such benefit or payment, conceals or fails to disclose such event with an intent fraudulently to secure such benefit or payment either in a greater amount or quantity than is due or when no such benefit or payment is authorized, (4) having made application to receive any such benefit or payment for the use and benefit of another and having received it, knowingly and willfully converts such benefit or payment or any part thereof to a use other than for the use and benefit of such other person, (5) presents or causes to be presented a claim for a physician's service for which payment may be made under a Federal health care program and knows that the individual who furnished the service was not licensed as a physician, or (6) for a fee knowingly
perturbation.justification: The relocation of the governmental rebates disclosure to the 'Confidentiality' section creates a structural flaw that undermines the transparency intended by 42 U.S.C. § 1320a-7b. While the changed text does not explicitly remove the disclosure requirement, placing it in a section concerning confidentiality arguably obscures it, potentially hindering its discoverability by relevant parties. The law snippet 2, '§1320a–7b. Criminal penalties for acts involving Federal health care programs,' outlines the importance of preventing fraud and abuse in federal healthcare programs. By placing the rebate information under 'Confidentiality,' the contract risks undermining this aim, as it is less likely to be noticed and properly acted upon by the appropriate entities.
